attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for her first antenatal visit at eight weeks gestation. This is her first pregnancy which was a surprise, however she is happy to continue the pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy multivitamins. She works as a retail assistant. Her partner is not present for the consultation.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.

She discloses to you that there has been conflict and a history of domestic violence with her current partner and she has had concerns for her safety at times.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the relationship between pregnancy and domestic violence?
options: Domestic violence decreases during pregnancy due to protective cultural factors
Pregnancy has no effect on domestic violence rates
Domestic violence often increases during pregnancy
Domestic violence risk only affects women with previous abuse history
Domestic violence in pregnancy only occurs in low socioeconomic groups
attributes.explanation:
Research consistently shows that domestic violence increases during pregnancy, with studies indicating that up to 1 in 6 women experience abuse during pregnancy. The physical, emotional, and financial stresses of pregnancy can trigger or escalate abusive behaviours. Additionally, pregnancy represents a period of increased vulnerability and dependence, which abusers may exploit to establish greater control.

The belief that domestic violence decreases during pregnancy due to protective cultural factors is incorrect. While some cultures place high value on protecting pregnant women, this does not translate to reduced rates of domestic violence.

It is incorrect that pregnancy has no effect on domestic violence rates. Multiple studies have demonstrated that pregnancy is a high-risk period for both the initiation and escalation of domestic violence.

The notion that domestic violence only affects women with previous abuse history is false. Many women experience their first episode of domestic violence during pregnancy, regardless of previous relationship dynamics.

The belief that domestic violence in pregnancy only occurs in low socioeconomic groups is a harmful myth. Domestic violence affects women across all socioeconomic, cultural, and educational backgrounds.

Healthcare providers should be aware that pregnancy represents a critical period for domestic violence screening and intervention. Risk factors include young age, unplanned pregnancy, financial stress, and substance use, but absence of risk factors does not exclude the possibility of domestic violence.
learningPoints: Domestic violence commonly begins or escalates during pregnancy, with up to 1 in 6 women experiencing abuse during this time.
Risk factors for domestic violence in pregnancy include young age, unplanned pregnancy, and financial stress.
Healthcare providers should maintain a high index of suspicion as pregnancy is a particularly vulnerable period.
references: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW). Family, domestic and sexual violence: Pregnant people [Internet]. Canberra: AIHW; 2023 [cited 2024 Nov 22]. Available from: https://www.aihw.gov.au/family-domestic-and-sexual-violence/population-groups/pregnant-people
